title: GitHub CLI and Actions Cheat Sheet
description: 180+ GitHub CLI commands and GitHub Actions workflow patterns. Includes a Failure Decoder for diagnosing broken workflow runs. Verified against official GitHub docs.

failureDecoder.title: GitHub Actions Failure Decoder
failureDecoder.subtitle: Describe what you see and find the real cause. These are the most searched GitHub Actions errors on Stack Overflow with combined views in the millions.

entries.symptom: My expression prints empty or blank value
entries.cause: You are mixing GitHub Actions expression syntax with shell variable syntax. Inside a run step dollar double-brace is evaluated first, then the shell runs. Using $name instead of ${{ inputs.name }} or vice versa produces an empty string with no error message.
entries.fix: Use ${{ inputs.name }} for GitHub Actions expressions and $NAME for shell environment variables. Never mix them in the same reference.

entries.symptom: github.sha gives wrong commit on pull_request trigger
entries.cause: On pull_request events github.sha is the merge commit SHA that GitHub creates automatically to test the merge, not the HEAD commit of your branch. This causes confusion when you tag Docker images or artifacts with github.sha and the SHA does not match what you see in git log.
entries.fix: Use github.event.pull_request.head.sha to get the actual HEAD SHA of the pull request branch. Use github.sha only on push events where it reliably refers to the commit that was pushed.

entries.symptom: Error 403 when writing to packages, pages, or pull requests
entries.cause: GitHub Actions workflows have read-only permissions by default since the May 2021 security update. The GITHUB_TOKEN can read everything but cannot write to packages, create releases, write to pull requests, or deploy to Pages without explicit permission grants.
entries.fix: Add a permissions block to your job or workflow specifying exactly what write access is needed. The minimum permission principle applies: only grant what the job actually needs.
entries.example:
permissions:
  contents: write
  packages: write
  pull-requests: write

entries.symptom: Secret is empty in a called reusable workflow
entries.cause: Secrets are not automatically inherited by called reusable workflows. The caller must explicitly pass secrets using the secrets keyword. Using secrets: inherit passes all secrets but this is a security risk in public repositories.
entries.fix: Explicitly pass each secret the called workflow needs using the secrets block in the workflow_call trigger of the called workflow and the with or secrets block in the caller.
entries.example:
# In caller workflow:
jobs:
  call-workflow:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/deploy.yml
    secrets:
      MY_SECRET: ${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }}

entries.symptom: Step output or step id reference returns empty value
entries.cause: GitHub Actions step ids and output names that contain hyphens cannot be referenced with dot notation in expressions. Hyphens in step ids must use bracket notation. Using a hyphenated step id with dot notation silently returns empty string.
entries.fix: Either rename step ids to use underscores instead of hyphens, or use bracket notation to reference outputs from hyphenated step ids.
entries.example:
# Problematic:
id: build-app
# Then: ${{ steps.build-app.outputs.version }} fails silently

# Fix option 1 - use underscore:
id: build_app
# Then: ${{ steps.build_app.outputs.version }} works

# Fix option 2 - bracket notation:
${{ steps["build-app"].outputs.version }}

entries.symptom: Composite action run steps fail with no shell specified error
entries.cause: Composite actions do not inherit a default shell from the caller workflow. Every run step inside a composite action must explicitly declare shell: bash or shell: sh. This is different from regular workflow jobs where the default shell is inherited.
entries.fix: Add shell: bash to every run step in your composite action definition.
entries.example:
steps:
  - name: Run script
    shell: bash
    run: echo 'hello'

entries.symptom: Workflow cannot access secrets on pull_request from a fork
entries.cause: For security reasons GitHub does not pass secrets to workflows triggered by pull_request events from fork repositories. This prevents malicious PRs from exfiltrating secrets. The actions/checkout@v7 update in June 2026 also changed how fork PR code is fetched by default.
entries.fix: Use the pull_request_target event instead of pull_request for workflows that need secrets. Be extremely careful with pull_request_target since it runs in the context of the base branch and can access secrets, so never checkout and run untrusted code from the fork in a pull_request_target workflow.

entries.symptom: Workflow stuck on Waiting for a runner to pick up this job
entries.cause: GitHub-hosted runners have capacity limits and during peak times jobs queue. This is more common with larger runner sizes. The free tier 2000 minutes per month can also cause delays if you are near the limit.
entries.fix: For critical workflows consider self-hosted runners. For non-critical workflows add a timeout-minutes to the job so stuck jobs fail fast rather than consuming billable minutes. Check your organization billing page to verify you have remaining minutes.

entries.symptom: Workflow runs duplicate times or old run completes after new run starts
entries.cause: Without concurrency configuration GitHub Actions runs every triggered workflow independently. On fast-push branches this means multiple runs of the same workflow overlap, wasting minutes at best and overwriting a newer deployment with a stale one at worst.
entries.fix: Add a concurrency block with cancel-in-progress: true to cancel older runs when a new one starts for the same branch.

entries.symptom: Secret value appears visible in workflow run logs
entries.cause: GitHub automatically masks secrets in logs when you use ${{ secrets.MY_SECRET }} directly. But if you assign a secret to an environment variable and then echo that variable, GitHub may not mask it. Base64-encoding or transforming a secret also removes the masking.
entries.fix: Never echo secrets directly. If you must log something for debugging use only a partial value or a hash. Use the add-mask workflow command to manually mask a derived value.
entries.example:
# To mask a derived value:
- run: echo "::add-mask::$MY_DERIVED_VALUE"
- run: echo "$MY_DERIVED_VALUE" # now masked

commands.title: Authenticate the GitHub CLI

flags.description: Reads token from stdin for CI use
Authenticates to a GitHub Enterprise instance

commands.useCase: Authenticate the GitHub CLI with your GitHub account before using any other gh commands.
commands.beginnerNote: Run gh auth status after logging in to confirm authentication worked correctly.

commands.title: Create a pull request

flags.description: Uses the branch name and commits as the PR title and body

commands.useCase: Create a pull request from the current branch directly from the terminal without opening the browser.
commands.beginnerNote: --fill is the most useful flag for quick PRs as it populates the title and body automatically from your commit messages.

commands.title: Merge a pull request

flags.description: Rebases commits onto the base branch
Deletes the head branch after merge

commands.useCase: Merge a pull request from the terminal after it has been approved.
commands.beginnerNote: --squash --delete-branch is the most common combination for keeping a clean main branch history.

commands.title: Watch a workflow run live

flags.description: Exits with a non-zero status if the run fails, useful in scripts

commands.useCase: Watch a GitHub Actions workflow run in real time from the terminal showing live status updates.

commands.title: Trigger a workflow on push
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow on every push to the main branch.
commands.beginnerNote: Always specify branch filters on push triggers or the workflow runs on every push to every branch in the repository which wastes runner minutes.

commands.title: Check current authentication status
commands.useCase: Check which GitHub account is currently authenticated and which scopes the token has.
commands.beginnerNote: Run this first when any gh command fails with authentication errors.

commands.title: Log out of a GitHub account
commands.useCase: Log out of the current GitHub account.

commands.title: Print the current auth token
commands.useCase: Print the current authentication token to stdout for use in scripts or to pass to other tools.
commands.beginnerNote: Never log the output of this command in CI as it prints the raw token.

commands.title: Set a gh CLI configuration value

flags.description: Sets config for a specific GitHub host

commands.useCase: Configure gh settings like preferred editor, git protocol, and browser.

commands.title: Read a gh CLI configuration value
commands.useCase: Check the current value of a gh configuration setting.

commands.title: Add OAuth scopes to an existing token
commands.useCase: Add new OAuth scopes to your existing token without re-logging in.

commands.title: Install a gh CLI extension
commands.useCase: Install a gh CLI extension to add new commands.

commands.title: List installed gh CLI extensions
commands.useCase: List all installed gh CLI extensions.

commands.title: Upgrade gh CLI extensions
commands.useCase: Upgrade all installed gh CLI extensions to their latest versions.

commands.title: Remove a gh CLI extension
commands.useCase: Uninstall a gh CLI extension.

commands.title: Create and clone a new repository

flags.description: Makes it internal to an org
Clones the repo locally after creating it

commands.useCase: Create a new GitHub repository and immediately clone it locally in one command.
commands.beginnerNote: Without --clone you only create the remote repo and still need to git clone it separately.

commands.title: Clone a repository by owner/name
commands.useCase: Clone a GitHub repository using the short owner/repo format without needing the full URL.

commands.title: Fork and clone a repository

flags.description: Adds the original repo as a remote named upstream

commands.useCase: Fork a repository and clone it locally in one command.

commands.title: View repository information

flags.description: Opens the repo in the browser

commands.useCase: View information about the current repository or open it in the browser.

commands.title: List repositories
commands.useCase: List repositories for your account or an organization.

commands.title: Delete a repository
commands.useCase: Permanently delete a GitHub repository.
commands.beginnerNote: Repository deletion is permanent and cannot be undone, all issues PRs and history are lost.

commands.title: Create an issue
commands.command: gh issue create --title "Bug: login fails" --body "Steps to reproduce..."

flags.description: Opens in browser to finish creating

commands.useCase: Create a GitHub issue from the terminal.
commands.beginnerNote: Use --web if you need rich formatting or file attachments.

commands.title: List issues
commands.useCase: List open issues in the current repository filtered by label.

commands.title: View an issue

flags.description: Opens the issue in the browser

commands.useCase: Read an issue in the terminal without opening the browser.

commands.title: Close an issue

flags.description: Sets the close reason as completed or not-planned

commands.useCase: Close an issue from the terminal with an optional comment.

commands.title: Reopen a closed issue
commands.useCase: Reopen a previously closed issue.

commands.title: Comment on an issue
commands.command: gh issue comment 123 --body "Looking into this now"
commands.useCase: Add a comment to an issue from the terminal.

commands.title: Edit issue metadata
commands.useCase: Edit issue metadata from the terminal.

commands.title: Pin an issue
commands.useCase: Pin an issue to the top of the repository issue list.

commands.title: Search issues
commands.command: gh search issues "memory leak" --repo owner/repo --state open

flags.description: Limits search to a specific repo

commands.useCase: Search for issues across repositories using GitHub search syntax.

commands.title: Create an issue label
commands.command: gh label create "priority:high" --color FF0000 --description "Needs immediate attention"
commands.useCase: Create a new issue label in the current repository.

commands.title: List repository labels
commands.useCase: List all labels in the current repository.

commands.title: List milestones
commands.useCase: List all milestones in the current repository.

commands.title: Sync a fork with upstream
commands.useCase: Sync a fork with its upstream repository.

commands.title: Open the current repo in a browser

flags.description: Prints the URL instead of opening it

commands.useCase: Open the current repository in the browser from any directory.

commands.title: List pull requests
commands.useCase: List pull requests in the current repository.

commands.title: View a pull request
commands.useCase: Read a pull request in the terminal.

commands.title: Check out a pull request branch
commands.useCase: Check out the branch of a pull request locally to test it.
commands.beginnerNote: This creates a local branch tracking the PR branch so you can run and test the code without manually finding and fetching the branch.

commands.title: Approve or request changes on a PR
commands.useCase: Approve or request changes on a pull request from the terminal.

commands.title: Comment on a pull request
commands.useCase: Add a comment to a pull request from the terminal.

commands.title: Edit pull request metadata
commands.useCase: Edit pull request metadata from the terminal.

commands.title: View a pull request diff
commands.useCase: View the diff of a pull request in the terminal.

commands.title: See relevant pull request status
commands.useCase: See the status of pull requests relevant to you, assigned to you, review requested, and your own open PRs.

commands.title: Mark a draft PR as ready
commands.useCase: Convert a draft pull request to ready for review.

commands.title: Close a pull request
commands.command: gh pr close 123 --comment "Closing in favour of #456" --delete-branch
commands.useCase: Close a pull request without merging it.

commands.title: Reopen a closed pull request
commands.useCase: Reopen a closed pull request.

commands.title: View pull request check status

flags.description: Watches check status until all checks complete
Sets check poll interval in seconds

commands.useCase: See the status of all required checks on a pull request.

commands.title: Enable auto-merge on a pull request
commands.useCase: Enable auto-merge on a PR so it merges automatically when all required checks pass and approvals are met.
commands.beginnerNote: Auto-merge only works if branch protection rules are enabled on the repository.

commands.title: Search pull requests
commands.useCase: Search for pull requests across repositories.

commands.title: Lock conversation on a pull request
commands.useCase: Lock conversation on a pull request to prevent further comments.

commands.title: Create a draft pull request with explicit title and body
commands.command: gh pr create --draft --title "WIP: new feature" --body "Work in progress"
commands.useCase: Create a draft PR with a specific title and body instead of letting --fill generate them from commits.

commands.title: Extract specific PR fields as JSON
commands.useCase: Get specific pull request fields as structured JSON for use in scripts.

commands.title: List your own open pull requests
commands.useCase: List only pull requests you authored, filtering out everyone else's.

commands.title: List recent workflow runs
commands.useCase: List recent workflow runs in the current repository.

commands.title: View a workflow run's details and logs
commands.useCase: View the details and logs of a specific workflow run.
commands.beginnerNote: gh run view with --log-failed is the fastest way to see only what failed without scrolling through all logs.

commands.title: Watch a specific workflow run

flags.description: Exits with non-zero status if the run fails

commands.useCase: Stream live status updates of a workflow run until it completes.

commands.title: Rerun a failed workflow run

flags.description: Enables debug logging for the rerun

commands.useCase: Rerun a failed workflow run without triggering a new push.
commands.beginnerNote: --failed-only is much faster than rerunning all jobs when only one job failed.

commands.title: Cancel a running workflow run
commands.useCase: Cancel a currently running workflow run.

commands.title: Delete a workflow run
commands.useCase: Delete a workflow run from the repository history.

commands.title: List workflow files
commands.useCase: List all workflow files in the current repository.

commands.title: Manually trigger a workflow

flags.description: Passes an input to a workflow_dispatch trigger
Runs the workflow on a specific branch or tag

commands.useCase: Manually trigger a workflow that has a workflow_dispatch trigger.
commands.beginnerNote: The workflow must have on: workflow_dispatch defined or this command fails.

commands.title: Re-enable a disabled workflow
commands.useCase: Re-enable a disabled workflow.

commands.title: Disable a workflow
commands.useCase: Disable a workflow from running.

commands.title: Set a repository secret

flags.description: Sets a secret at the environment level
Sets a secret at the organization level
Limits which repos can access an org secret

commands.useCase: Set a GitHub Actions secret in the repository.
commands.beginnerNote: Secret values are write-only after setting, you cannot read them back through the API or CLI.

commands.title: List repository secrets

flags.description: Lists secrets for a specific environment

commands.useCase: List all secrets defined in the repository.
commands.beginnerNote: This only lists secret names not values, GitHub never exposes secret values after they are set.

commands.title: Delete a repository secret
commands.useCase: Delete a secret from the repository.

commands.title: Set a repository variable
commands.useCase: Set a GitHub Actions variable which unlike secrets is not masked in logs.
commands.beginnerNote: Variables are visible in logs unlike secrets, never store sensitive values as variables.

commands.title: Create a release with assets
commands.command: gh release create v1.0.0 --title "Version 1.0.0" --notes "Release notes here" dist/*.zip
commands.useCase: Create a GitHub release and upload release assets in one command.

commands.title: List releases
commands.useCase: List all releases in the repository.

commands.title: Minimal workflow structure
commands.command:
name: CI

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Run tests
        run: npm test
commands.useCase: A minimal GitHub Actions workflow that runs on push to main.
commands.beginnerNote: Always pin actions to a specific version tag like v4 not just v4 to avoid unexpected breaking changes when the action updates.

commands.title: Choose a runner environment

flags.description: Uses the most recent Ubuntu runner

commands.useCase: Specify which runner environment to use for a job.
commands.beginnerNote: ubuntu-latest is the cheapest runner at 1x the minute multiplier, Windows runners cost 2x and macOS runners cost 10x your billing minutes.

commands.title: Understand uses versus run
commands.command:
steps:
  - uses: actions/checkout@v4  # runs a pre-built action
  - run: echo 'hello world'  # runs a shell command
commands.useCase: Understand the difference between uses which calls a pre-built action and run which executes a shell command.
commands.beginnerNote: uses and run cannot both be set on the same step.

commands.title: Set an environment variable for one step
commands.command:
steps:
  - name: Print var
    env:
      MY_VAR: hello
    run: echo "$MY_VAR"
commands.useCase: Pass environment variables to a specific step.
commands.beginnerNote: Environment variables set with env at the step level are only available in that step. Use env at the job or workflow level to share variables across steps.

commands.title: Pass a value between jobs
commands.command:
jobs:
  build:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    outputs:
      version: ${{ steps.get-version.outputs.version }}
    steps:
      - id: get-version
        run: echo "version=1.0.0" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
commands.useCase: Pass a value from one job to another job that depends on it.
commands.beginnerNote: Use echo key=value >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT to set step outputs. The old syntax of set-output is deprecated and will stop working.

commands.title: Set and read a step output
commands.command:
steps:
  - id: my_step
    run: echo "result=success" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
  - run: echo ${{ steps.my_step.outputs.result }}
commands.useCase: Set and read an output from one step in subsequent steps of the same job.
commands.beginnerNote: Step ids must use underscores not hyphens if you want to reference them with dot notation in expressions, hyphens silently return empty.

commands.title: Persist an environment variable across steps
commands.useCase: Set an environment variable that persists for all subsequent steps in the same job.
commands.beginnerNote: Variables set with GITHUB_ENV are available in all subsequent steps but not in the current step where they are set.

commands.title: Set a job timeout
commands.useCase: Set a maximum runtime for a job to prevent stuck jobs from consuming all your runner minutes.
commands.beginnerNote: Without timeout-minutes a job runs for up to 6 hours by default, always set a realistic timeout to limit wasted minutes.

commands.title: Allow a step to fail without failing the job
commands.command:
steps:
  - name: Flaky test
    continue-on-error: true
    run: npm run flaky-test
commands.useCase: Allow a step to fail without failing the entire job.
commands.beginnerNote: continue-on-error: true means the step outcome is failure but the job continues. The overall job still reports success which can hide real failures if overused.

commands.title: Run a step in a specific directory
commands.command:
steps:
  - name: Run in subdirectory
    working-directory: ./frontend
    run: npm install
commands.useCase: Run a step in a specific directory without manually cd-ing into it.

commands.title: Name a workflow
commands.useCase: Give a workflow a descriptive name that appears in the Actions tab instead of the filename.

commands.title: Set default shell and directory for all steps
commands.useCase: Set a default shell and working directory that applies to every run step in a job without repeating it on each step.

commands.title: Trigger on push with branch filters
commands.useCase: Trigger only on pushes to main or any release branch.
commands.beginnerNote: Without branch filters every push to every branch triggers the workflow.

commands.title: Trigger on pull request events
commands.command:
on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - main
    types:
      - opened
      - synchronize
      - reopened
commands.useCase: Trigger on pull requests targeting main that are opened updated or reopened.
commands.beginnerNote: The default types for pull_request are opened synchronize and reopened so specifying them explicitly is optional but makes intent clear.

commands.title: Trigger on a cron schedule
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow on a schedule using cron syntax.
commands.beginnerNote: Scheduled workflows only run on the default branch. Also the cron timezone is UTC so adjust accordingly for your local time.

commands.title: Add a manual trigger with inputs
commands.command:
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      environment:
        description: 'Deploy environment'
        required: true
        default: 'staging'
        type: choice
        options:
          - staging
          - production
commands.useCase: Add a manual trigger button to a workflow with optional input parameters.
commands.beginnerNote: The workflow must be on the default branch or a specific branch to be triggerable from the Actions tab UI.

commands.title: Make a workflow reusable
commands.command:
on:
  workflow_call:
    inputs:
      environment:
        required: true
        type: string
    secrets:
      DEPLOY_KEY:
        required: true
commands.useCase: Make a workflow reusable so it can be called from other workflows.
commands.beginnerNote: Secrets passed to a called workflow must be explicitly declared in the workflow_call trigger.

commands.title: Trigger when a release is published
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when a GitHub release is published.
commands.beginnerNote: Use published not created since draft releases trigger created but not published.

commands.title: Trigger only when specific paths change
commands.useCase: Only trigger a workflow when specific files or directories change.
commands.beginnerNote: The exclamation mark prefix excludes paths. If only excluded paths change the workflow does not trigger.

commands.title: Trigger with secrets access on fork PRs
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow with access to secrets on pull requests from forks.
commands.beginnerNote: Never checkout and run code from the fork PR in a pull_request_target workflow as it has access to secrets. This is a serious security risk documented in the GitHub security advisory.

commands.title: Trigger on issue or PR comments
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when someone comments on an issue or pull request.
commands.beginnerNote: This fires on comments to both issues and pull requests, check github.event.issue.pull_request to determine if the comment is on a PR.

commands.title: Trigger only on version tags
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow only when a version tag is pushed.

commands.title: Trigger when a branch or tag is deleted
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when a branch or tag is deleted, useful for cleanup automation like removing a matching preview environment.

commands.title: Trigger when a branch or tag is created
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when a branch or tag is created.

commands.title: Trigger when the repository is forked
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow whenever someone forks the repository.

commands.title: Trigger when someone stars the repository
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when someone stars the repository, sometimes used for fun community automations like a thank-you comment bot.

commands.title: Trigger on GitHub Discussions activity
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when a new GitHub Discussion is created.

commands.title: Trigger when a check run completes
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when a check run completes, useful for chaining automation after a third-party check reports its result.

commands.title: Trigger when a deployment status changes
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow when a deployment status changes, such as running smoke tests after a deployment reports success.

commands.title: Trigger a workflow after another workflow completes
commands.useCase: Chain workflows together so one workflow starts automatically after another named workflow finishes.

commands.title: Trigger on all branches except matching ones
commands.useCase: Trigger a workflow on pushes to any branch except ones matching a pattern, such as documentation branches.
commands.beginnerNote: branches and branches-ignore cannot both be used in the same trigger block.

commands.title: Skip triggering when only ignored paths change
commands.useCase: Skip triggering a workflow when only documentation or other ignored files change.

commands.title: Get the commit SHA
commands.useCase: Get the full commit SHA that triggered the workflow.
commands.beginnerNote: On pull_request events this is the SHA of the auto-generated merge commit not your branch HEAD. Use github.event.pull_request.head.sha to get the actual PR branch commit SHA.

commands.title: Get the full ref
commands.useCase: Get the full ref that triggered the workflow such as refs/heads/main or refs/tags/v1.0.0.
commands.beginnerNote: github.ref includes the refs/heads/ prefix. Use github.ref_name to get just the branch or tag name without the prefix.

commands.title: Get just the branch or tag name
commands.useCase: Get just the branch name or tag name without the refs/heads/ or refs/tags/ prefix.
commands.beginnerNote: This is almost always what you want instead of github.ref when you need the branch name.

commands.title: Get source and target branch on a PR
commands.command:
# On pull_request events:
# github.head_ref = source branch (your feature branch)
# github.base_ref = target branch (usually main)
commands.useCase: Get the source and target branch names on pull request events.
commands.beginnerNote: github.head_ref and github.base_ref are only populated on pull_request events. On push events they are empty.

commands.title: Get who triggered the workflow
commands.useCase: Get the username of the person or bot that triggered the workflow.

commands.title: Get the triggering event name
commands.useCase: Get the name of the event that triggered the workflow such as push pull_request or schedule.

commands.title: Get the unique run ID
commands.useCase: Get the unique ID of the current workflow run for building artifact names or URLs.

commands.title: Get the owner and repository name
commands.useCase: Get the owner and repository name in the format owner/repo.

commands.title: Run a step conditionally
commands.useCase: Run a step or job only when a specific condition is met.
commands.beginnerNote: In if conditions you do not use double curly braces. The expression is evaluated directly without dollar sign double-brace wrapping.

commands.title: Check for a label with contains
commands.useCase: Check if a PR has a specific label before running a deployment step.

commands.title: Run a step only on failure
commands.useCase: Run a step only when a previous step has failed, useful for sending notifications on failure.
commands.beginnerNote: if: failure() still requires all previous steps to have completed. Use always() if you want the step to run even if the job is cancelled.

commands.title: Reference an environment variable in an expression
commands.useCase: Reference an environment variable set at the workflow job or step level inside an expression.

commands.title: Reference a secret in an expression
commands.useCase: Reference a repository or organization secret inside an expression or step.
commands.beginnerNote: Secrets are masked in logs automatically when referenced as ${{ secrets.NAME }} but derived values or manually echoed secrets may not be masked.

commands.title: Reference a repository variable
commands.useCase: Reference a repository or organization variable which unlike secrets is visible in logs.
commands.beginnerNote: Use vars for non-sensitive configuration values and secrets for anything sensitive.

commands.title: Access another job's output
commands.useCase: Access output values from a job that the current job depends on.
commands.beginnerNote: The needs context is only available in jobs that declare an explicit needs dependency.

commands.title: Get information about the runner
commands.useCase: Get information about the runner executing the current job such as the operating system.

commands.title: Access the full event payload
commands.useCase: Access data from the webhook event payload that triggered the workflow, such as the commit message on a push event.

commands.title: Convert a context to JSON for debugging
commands.useCase: Convert a context object to a formatted JSON string, useful for printing the full event payload when debugging a workflow.

commands.title: Build a formatted string
commands.useCase: Build a formatted string from multiple context values, similar to a template string.

commands.title: Run a step regardless of job status
commands.useCase: Run a step regardless of whether previous steps failed, useful for cleanup steps that must always execute.
commands.beginnerNote: always() still respects a cancelled workflow in some edge cases, pair it with a timeout on the cleanup step itself for full reliability.

commands.title: Access a repository secret
commands.useCase: Access a repository-level secret in a workflow step.
commands.beginnerNote: Secrets set at the repository level are available to all workflows in that repository by default.

commands.title: Scope secrets to a deployment environment
commands.command:
jobs:
  deploy:
    environment: production
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
commands.useCase: Use environment-level secrets that are only available to jobs that specify that environment, allowing different secrets for staging and production.
commands.beginnerNote: Environment secrets require the job to declare environment: environment-name and the environment must be configured in repository settings.

commands.title: Grant write permissions to GITHUB_TOKEN
commands.command:
permissions:
  contents: read
  packages: write
  pull-requests: write
commands.useCase: Grant specific write permissions to the GITHUB_TOKEN for a workflow or job.
commands.beginnerNote: Without explicit write permissions the GITHUB_TOKEN only has read access since the May 2021 security update. This is the most common cause of 403 errors in workflows.

commands.title: Use the automatic GitHub token
commands.useCase: Use the automatically provided GitHub token to authenticate API calls and push to the repository from within a workflow.
commands.beginnerNote: The GITHUB_TOKEN expires when the workflow finishes. It cannot trigger other workflows to prevent infinite loops.

commands.title: Set a shared organization secret
commands.command: gh secret set MY_SECRET --org myorg --body 'value' --repos repo1,repo2
commands.useCase: Set a secret at the organization level that can be shared across multiple repositories.

commands.title: Set workflow-level environment variables
commands.useCase: Set environment variables at the workflow job or step level.
commands.beginnerNote: Use vars context for repository variables set in settings and env key for hardcoded values in the workflow file.

commands.title: Grant OIDC token permission
commands.useCase: Grant OIDC token permission so a workflow can authenticate to a cloud provider like AWS or Azure without storing long-lived credentials as secrets.

commands.title: Grant read access to workflow run data
commands.useCase: Grant read access to workflow run information, needed by workflows that query other runs through the API.

commands.title: Grant deployment write access
commands.useCase: Grant permission to create and update deployments, needed by workflows that mark GitHub deployment status.

commands.title: Grant issue write access
commands.useCase: Grant permission for a workflow to comment on or label issues automatically.

commands.title: Grant read access to all scopes
commands.useCase: Grant read access to all permission scopes at once using the shorthand instead of listing each scope individually.

commands.title: Explicitly grant zero permissions
commands.useCase: Explicitly grant zero permissions to the GITHUB_TOKEN, the most restrictive setting for jobs that only need to run scripts with no repository access.

commands.title: Use a personal access token to trigger other workflows
commands.useCase: Use a personal access token instead of GITHUB_TOKEN when a workflow needs to push a commit that should trigger other workflows, since GITHUB_TOKEN cannot do that.
commands.beginnerNote: Store the personal access token as a secret and rotate it periodically, since unlike GITHUB_TOKEN it does not expire automatically.

commands.title: Set a secret for Dependabot
commands.useCase: Set a secret specifically scoped for Dependabot to use when it needs to authenticate to a private package registry.

commands.title: Get structured secret metadata
commands.useCase: Get structured secret metadata for auditing which secrets exist and when they were last updated.

commands.title: Make a job wait for another job
commands.command:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
  deploy:
    needs: test
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
commands.useCase: Make one job wait for another to complete successfully before starting.
commands.beginnerNote: needs accepts a single string or an array of job ids. A job that needs another only starts if all needed jobs succeed.

commands.title: Run a job across a matrix of variables
commands.useCase: Run a job multiple times with different combinations of variables such as testing across multiple operating systems and Node.js versions.
commands.beginnerNote: Matrix creates a job for every combination so 2 os values times 3 node versions creates 6 parallel jobs.

commands.title: Add extra variables to specific matrix combinations
commands.command:
strategy:
  matrix:
    os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
    include:
      - os: windows-latest
        npm-flags: --legacy-peer-deps
commands.useCase: Add extra variables to specific matrix combinations without creating new combinations.

commands.title: Remove specific combinations from a matrix
commands.command:
strategy:
  matrix:
    os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
    node: [18, 20]
    exclude:
      - os: windows-latest
        node: 18
commands.useCase: Remove specific combinations from the matrix.

commands.title: Let all matrix jobs finish even if one fails
commands.command:
strategy:
  fail-fast: false
  matrix:
    os: [ubuntu-latest, windows-latest]
commands.useCase: Allow all matrix jobs to complete even if one fails, useful when you want to see results across all environments.
commands.beginnerNote: fail-fast defaults to true which cancels all remaining matrix jobs when one fails.

commands.title: Cancel in-progress runs on new push
commands.useCase: Cancel any in-progress run of the same workflow on the same branch when a new run starts.
commands.beginnerNote: Without concurrency configuration multiple runs of the same workflow can run simultaneously on the same branch leading to race conditions and wasted minutes.

commands.title: Run a service container alongside a job
commands.command:
services:
  postgres:
    image: postgres:15
    env:
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: password
    ports:
      - 5432:5432
    options: >-
      --health-cmd pg_isready
      --health-interval 10s
      --health-timeout 5s
      --health-retries 5
commands.useCase: Run a service container alongside a job for integration testing with a real database or other service.

commands.title: Limit how many matrix jobs run at once
commands.useCase: Limit how many matrix jobs run simultaneously, useful when a shared resource like a database only supports a few concurrent connections.

commands.title: Run an entire job conditionally
commands.useCase: Run an entire job only when a condition is met, such as only deploying from the main branch.

commands.title: Associate a job with a deployment environment
commands.command:
jobs:
  deploy:
    environment:
      name: production
      url: https://example.com
commands.useCase: Associate a job with a deployment environment so it shows up with a link in the repository's Environments UI.

commands.title: Run a job inside a Docker container
commands.command:
jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    container: node:20
commands.useCase: Run a job inside a specific Docker container instead of directly on the runner, ensuring a consistent environment regardless of what the runner image includes.

commands.title: Build a matrix dynamically from a previous job
commands.useCase: Build a matrix dynamically from JSON produced by a previous job, useful when the set of things to test is computed at runtime rather than hardcoded.

commands.title: Queue runs instead of cancelling them
commands.useCase: Queue runs instead of cancelling them, ensuring only one deployment happens at a time without discarding any triggered run.
commands.beginnerNote: Without cancel-in-progress, concurrency queues runs one after another rather than cancelling the older one.

commands.title: Wait for multiple jobs to complete
commands.useCase: Wait for multiple jobs to complete successfully before starting a job that depends on all of them.

commands.title: Name matrix jobs descriptively
commands.command:
jobs:
  test:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        node: [18, 20]
    name: Test (Node ${{ matrix.node }})
commands.useCase: Give each matrix job a descriptive name in the Actions UI instead of the default auto-generated name.

commands.title: Use a matrix variable as the runner label
commands.useCase: Use a matrix variable directly as the runs-on value so each matrix combination runs on the operating system specified in that combination.

commands.title: Call a reusable workflow
commands.command:
jobs:
  deploy:
    uses: ./.github/workflows/deploy.yml
    with:
      environment: production
    secrets:
      DEPLOY_KEY: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}
commands.useCase: Call a reusable workflow from another workflow passing inputs and secrets.
commands.beginnerNote: Secrets are not automatically inherited by called workflows, each secret must be explicitly passed.

commands.title: Choose between a reusable workflow and a composite action
commands.command:
# Reusable workflow: separate .yml file, can have multiple jobs
# Composite action: action.yml in a folder, flat list of steps only
commands.useCase: Understand when to use a reusable workflow versus a composite action.
commands.beginnerNote: Use reusable workflows when you need multiple jobs or job-level features like services and matrix. Use composite actions when you need a reusable set of steps within a single job.

commands.title: Define a composite action
commands.command:
# In .github/actions/my-action/action.yml:
name: My Action
description: Does something
inputs:
  param:
    required: true
runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - shell: bash
      run: echo ${{ inputs.param }}
commands.useCase: Define a composite action that can be called with uses in any workflow.
commands.beginnerNote: Every run step in a composite action must have shell: bash explicitly set as composite actions do not inherit a default shell.

commands.title: Define outputs for a reusable workflow
commands.command:
on:
  workflow_call:
    outputs:
      result:
        value: ${{ jobs.build.outputs.result }}
commands.useCase: Define outputs a reusable workflow returns to its caller so the caller can use values computed inside the called workflow.

commands.title: Define an output from a composite action
commands.useCase: Define an output from a composite action so the calling workflow can read a value the action computed.

commands.title: Set a default value for a composite action input
commands.command:
inputs:
  environment:
    required: false
    default: staging
commands.useCase: Set a default value for a composite action input so callers do not need to specify it every time.

commands.title: Call a reusable workflow with a matrix
commands.command:
jobs:
  call:
    strategy:
      matrix:
        env: [staging, production]
    uses: ./.github/workflows/deploy.yml
    with:
      environment: ${{ matrix.env }}
commands.useCase: Call a reusable workflow multiple times using a matrix, such as deploying to staging and production in parallel.

commands.title: Call a reusable workflow from another repository
commands.useCase: Call a reusable workflow that lives in a different repository, useful for sharing standard CI patterns across an organization.
commands.beginnerNote: Pin the ref to a tag or commit SHA instead of a branch like main for production use, since a branch can change unexpectedly.

commands.title: Define an action that runs in a Docker container
commands.useCase: Define an action that runs inside a Docker container built from a Dockerfile instead of as composite steps.

commands.title: Define a JavaScript action entrypoint
commands.useCase: Define a JavaScript action's entrypoint file, the fastest starting action type since it does not require building a Docker image.

commands.title: Pass all caller secrets to a called workflow
commands.useCase: Pass every secret available to the caller workflow into the called reusable workflow without listing each one individually.
commands.beginnerNote: secrets: inherit is convenient but grants the called workflow access to every secret the caller has, which is a security risk in public repositories or when calling third-party workflows.

commands.title: Call another action from inside a composite action
commands.command:
runs:
  using: composite
  steps:
    - uses: actions/setup-node@v4
      with:
        node-version: 20
    - shell: bash
      run: npm install
commands.useCase: Call other actions from inside a composite action's steps, combining multiple existing actions into one reusable unit.

commands.title: Cache dependencies between runs
commands.command:
- uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.npm
    key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
    restore-keys: |
      ${{ runner.os }}-node-
commands.useCase: Cache dependencies between workflow runs to dramatically speed up builds.
commands.beginnerNote: The cache key must change when dependencies change. Using hashFiles on your lock file ensures the cache is invalidated when packages are updated.

commands.title: Upload a build artifact
commands.command:
- uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: build-output
    path: dist/
    retention-days: 7
commands.useCase: Save build outputs or test reports so they can be downloaded from the workflow run or used in a subsequent job.

commands.title: Download a build artifact
commands.command:
- uses: actions/download-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: build-output
    path: dist/
commands.useCase: Download an artifact uploaded by a previous job in the same workflow run.

commands.title: Enable verbose debug logging
commands.command:
# Set repository secret:
ACTIONS_STEP_DEBUG = true
ACTIONS_RUNNER_DEBUG = true
commands.useCase: Enable verbose debug logging for all steps and the runner in a workflow run.
commands.beginnerNote: Debug logs can expose sensitive values, disable after debugging by removing the secrets.

commands.title: Manually mask a value in logs
commands.useCase: Manually mask a value in workflow logs so it appears as asterisks even if it was derived from a secret and therefore not automatically masked.
commands.beginnerNote: Always mask derived secret values because GitHub only automatically masks the original secret value not values computed from it.

commands.title: Target a self-hosted runner by label
commands.useCase: Target a self-hosted runner with specific labels for jobs that need custom hardware, software, or network access.
commands.beginnerNote: Self-hosted runners must have the GitHub Actions runner application installed and registered with the repository or organization.

commands.title: Open an SSH session into a failed runner
commands.command:
- uses: mxschmitt/action-tmate@v3
  if: failure()
  with:
    limit-access-to-actor: true
commands.useCase: Open an interactive SSH session into the runner when a workflow fails for live debugging.
commands.beginnerNote: The tmate session keeps the runner occupied until you disconnect or the timeout is reached which consumes runner minutes.

commands.title: Use built-in caching in setup-node
commands.command:
- uses: actions/setup-node@v4
  with:
    node-version: 20
    cache: npm
commands.useCase: Use the built-in caching option in setup-node instead of manually configuring actions/cache, which is simpler and covers the common case.

commands.title: Skip a step when the cache was already restored
commands.command:
- id: cache
  uses: actions/cache@v4
  with:
    path: ~/.npm
    key: npm-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }}
- if: steps.cache.outputs.cache-hit != 'true'
  run: npm ci
commands.useCase: Skip an expensive install step entirely when the cache step reports a hit, saving even the time it takes to run a no-op install.

commands.title: Get a per-job temporary directory
commands.useCase: Get a temporary directory path that is unique per job and cleaned up automatically, useful for scratch files that should not be cached or committed.

commands.title: Rerun a workflow with debug logging from the CLI
commands.useCase: Rerun a workflow with debug logging enabled directly from the CLI without setting repository secrets first.

commands.title: Run GitHub Actions workflows locally
commands.useCase: Run GitHub Actions workflows locally for fast iteration without pushing commits, using the third-party act tool.
commands.beginnerNote: act runs workflows in Docker containers that approximate but do not perfectly match GitHub-hosted runners, so treat it as a fast local check rather than a full replacement for a real run.

commands.title: Write a custom job summary
commands.useCase: Write custom markdown to the workflow run summary page, useful for surfacing test results or deployment links without digging through logs.

commands.title: Get structured run data for scripting
commands.useCase: Get structured workflow run data as JSON for use in scripts that need to check recent run status programmatically.

commands.title: Target a self-hosted runner group
commands.useCase: Target a specific runner group instead of individual labels, common in organizations that manage multiple pools of self-hosted runners.

commands.title: List GitHub Actions caches
commands.useCase: List all GitHub Actions caches stored for the repository to see what is consuming cache storage.
